title: Reversing Democratic Decay in Hungary
content:
Hungary is poised to become a critical test case for reversing democratic decay in modern Europe. For years, Prime Minister Viktor Orban reveled in his role as the champion of a growing band of populist nationalists, systematically chipping away at Hungary’s independent institutions. However, his recent crushing defeat in the general election signals a profound shift in the country's political landscape. The triumphant and solidly conservative winner, Peter Magyar, is now being hailed by centrists and progressives as a model for restoring democratic norms. Following the election, Magyar’s party secured a commanding two-thirds majority in parliament, giving him the legal power to amend the constitution and dismantle the entrenched controls of the previous regime.

The election results underscore that voters are ultimately more concerned with corruption and economic competence than with warnings about inchoate tyranny. During his campaign, Magyar repeatedly highlighted how Orban and his cronies had enriched themselves through rigged public contracts and the diversion of European Union subsidies. Consequently, Hungarian voters have mandated Magyar to restore the rule of law, revive the struggling economy, and eradicate systemic corruption. Because the old political machine still controls the supreme court, media, and civil service, Magyar must be both ruthless and swift in dismantling these institutional hurdles. However, he must also remain magnanimous, ensuring that the diverse coalition of opposition groups that supported him shares in the new Hungary.

Furthermore, the election sent a clear message that Hungarians are weary of foreign interference from all sides. By rejecting attempts at influence from both Eastern powers and Western political figures, a solid majority demonstrated their desire to remain firmly within the European Union. The responsibility for putting the country back on track now falls entirely upon Magyar, who defected from Orban’s camp only two years ago. Now that Hungarians have elected a government bound to abide by the law, Europe should quickly unlock the billions of euros it had previously withheld. Liberating these funds will be crucial as Magyar embarks on the awesome task of rebuilding his nation's democratic foundations.
